evidence: The DB text at lines 312-314 contains '3 U.S. 269, 273' but the official PDF source shows the correct U.S. Reports citation '3 U.S. 386, 390'. Calder v. Bull is 3 U.S. (Dall.) 386 (1798), a foundational case on ex post facto law. The page numbers 269 and 273 do not match any legitimate edition of this case. The source PDF clearly shows the correct pinpoint 'Calder v. Bull, 3 U.S. 386, 390 (1798))' on line 246. This is an OCR or transcription error in the DB ingestion process.
note: Famous constitutional case citation; OCR misread 386 as 269 and 390 as 273. Occurred within quotation block discussing ex post facto constitutional principle.

evidence: The In re Carter citation is broken across 5 lines in the DB (after Carter,→newline→2019 ND 112, ¶ 3→newline→,→newline→924→newline→N.W.2d 112→newline→,) with improper spacing. The PDF source shows the citation properly formatted on two lines: 'In re Carter, 2019 ND 112, ¶ 3, 924' on one line and 'N.W.2d 112,' on the next. This is a line-breaking artifact from PDF ingestion that disrupts the readability and proper formatting of the legal citation.
note: This is the only substantive content error. All word-level content matches perfectly with the PDF source when normalized. The DB metadata header (# Matter of Wolff) and formatting differences are intentional and not editorial issues.
